author: @DaleOwens1
text:
awesome, graphene should be very good for planer speakers. lower ac impedance higher dc resistance  gives higher fidelity.

an electromagnet ran at high amperage low voltage dc will provide a powerful magnetic field.
tip; powder some iron and lightly dust the ink before it dries you'll have better response. ;)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe mate - I'll give it a go

replies.author: @DaleOwens1
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering some of the highest quality inductance coils for speaker crossovers are made using powdered ferrite that's been centered, baked together. also a lot of speakers use ferro fluid in the magnetic gap. 

I've been wondering what the effects of a diamagnetic material layered to a ferromagnetic material would be, with a magnetic field applied.

author: @waynegnarlie1
text: what kind of load does the speaker present to the amplifier?  If high resistance, maybe use the amplifier to drive with the 8 ohm side of a cheap 70V audio line transformer and drive the speaker with the 50ohm(70.7V) side output of the transformer.  Could potentially increase the volume( ~8db).

author: @scotttovey
text:
From what I remember about speaker design, the coil is wrapped around the magnet and also glued to the to the speaker face (paper). Thus the magnet is inside the coil of wire.

When the signal goes into the coil, it reacts to the magnet. I think that the coil winding around the circumference of the magnet causes larger oscillations and that in turn gives a louder volume.

OK, I picked up the computer speaker I had sitting on the coffee table and it appears to have the coil inside a metal housing with a ring magnet on the outside of that. The end wires of the coil are glued or epoxied to the speaker face.

I think that the coil winding around the inside or outside of the magnet is where it gets the greater volume. 

Maybe this design is simply more efficient that others and the actual volume of the speaker is determined by the amount of energy coursing through it.

replies.author: @waynegnarlie1
replies.text: Good suggestions to try!  Impedance matching the amplifier to the speaker would probably help as well.  Im guessing the painted speaker has a several hundred ohm resistance. Thought Rob could use a 70.7V audio line transformer to drive the speaker with 50 ohms output which would  increase the voltage dramatically and could get more power into the sheet.  Appropriate series-one reversed connected zener diodes connected across the amplifier output recommended to prevent transformer back EMF from blowing the amplifier's output stage.

author: @ZeroInDaHouse
text: Cool video. Seeing as you are interested in getting energy from the environment I highly recommend you look at this paper: "Water-evaporation-induced electricity with nanostructured carbon materials". It uses the streaming potential and water evaporation (essentially energy from the sun) to create electrical energy directly. I'm not sure how well the ink would work if you repeated their experiment.
